perturbation.original_text: 12. This Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of California without reference to conflict of laws principles.
perturbation.changed_text: 12. This Agreement shall be governed by the laws of California, but federal common law principles of contract interpretation shall take precedence.
perturbation.explanation: This change introduces a contradiction by stating that while California law governs, federal common law principles override it. This is problematic because contract interpretation is generally governed by state law, not federal common law.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Erie Railroad Co. v. Tompkins, 304 U.S. 64 (1938)
perturbation.law_citation: Erie R. Co. v. Tompkins, 304 U.S. 64
perturbation.law_explanation: The *Erie* doctrine generally requires federal courts sitting in diversity jurisdiction to apply state substantive law (including state common law rules of contract interpretation) to claims arising under state law. By stipulating that federal common law principles supersede California law, the clause directly contradicts the established principle that state law governs such matters in diversity cases.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: ERIE R. CO. v. TOMPKINS. | Supreme Court | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you ERIE R. CO. v. TOMPKINS. Supreme Court 304 U.S. 64 58 S.Ct. 817 82 L.Ed. 1188 ERIE R. CO. v. TOMPKINS. * No. 367. Argued Jan. 31, 1938. Decided April 25, 1938. Messrs. Theodore Kiendl, Harold W. Bissell, and William C. Cannon, all of New York City, for petitioner. [Argument of Counsel from pages 65-67 intentionally omitted] Messrs. Fred H. Rees, Alexander L. Strouse, and Bernard G. Nemeroff, all of New York City (Bernard Kaufman and William Walsh, both of New York City, and Aaron L. Danzig, of Jamaica, L.I., on the brief) for respondent. [Argument of Counsel from Page 68 intentionally omitted] Mr. Justice BRANDEIS delivered the opinion of the Court. 1 The question for decision is whether the oft-challenged doctrine of Swift v. Tyson 1 shall now be disapproved. 2 Tompkins, a citizen of Pennsylvania, was injured on a dark night by a passing freight train of the Erie Railroad Company while walking along its right of way at Hughestown in that state. He claimed that the accident occurred through negligence in the operation, or maintenance, of the train; that he was rightfully on the premises as licensee because on a commonly used beaten footpath which ran for a short distance alongside the tracks; and that he was struck by something which looked like a door projecting from one of the moving cars. To enforce that claim he brought an action in the federal court for Southern New York, which had jurisdiction because the company is a corporation of that state. It denied liability; and the case was tried by a jury. 3 The Erie insisted that its duty to Tompkins was no greater than that owed to a trespasser. It contended, among other things, that its duty to Tompkins, and hence its liability, should be determined in accordance with the Pennsylvania law; that under the law of Pennsylvania, as declared by its highest court, persons who use pathways along the railroad right of way—that is, a longitudinal pathway as distinguished from a crossing—are to be deemed trespassers; and that the railroad is not liable for injuries
perturbation.justification: The modified text creates a legal contradiction because it stipulates that federal common law principles supersede California law in the interpretation of the agreement. The Erie doctrine, as indicated in Scraped Law Snippet 2 (*ERIE R. CO. v. TOMPKINS.*), mandates that federal courts apply state substantive law, including state common law rules of contract interpretation, in diversity jurisdiction cases. By prioritizing federal common law over California law, the changed text directly contradicts this established principle, making the agreement potentially unenforceable.

perturbation.original_text: 1. As used herein, "CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION" shall mean any and all technical and non-technical information provided by either party to the other, including but not limited to (a) patent and patent applications, (b) trade secret, and (c) proprietary information, ideas, techniques, sketches, drawings, works of authorship, models, inventions, know-how, processes, apparatuses, equipment, algorithms, software programs, software source documents, and formulae related to the current, future, and proposed products and services of each of the parties, and including, without limitation, their respective information concerning research, experimental work, development, design details and specifications, engineering, financial information, procurement requirements, purchasing, manufacturing, customer lists, investors, employees, business and contractual relationships, business forecasts, sales and merchandising, marketing plans and information the disclosing party provides regarding third parties.
perturbation.changed_text: 1. As used herein, "CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION" shall mean any and all technical and non-technical information provided by either party to the other, including but not limited to (a) patent and patent applications, (b) trade secret, and (c) proprietary information, ideas, techniques, sketches, drawings, works of authorship, models, inventions, know-how, processes, apparatuses, equipment, algorithms, software programs, software source documents, and formulae related to the current, future, and proposed products and services of each of the parties, and including, without limitation, their respective information concerning research, experimental work, development, design details and specifications, engineering, financial information, procurement requirements, purchasing, manufacturing, customer lists, investors, employees, business and contractual relationships, business forecasts, sales and merchandising, marketing plans and information the disclosing party provides regarding third parties. Confidential Information does not include information shared in good faith.
perturbation.explanation: Adding the clause 'Confidential Information does not include information shared in good faith' is problematic. Trade secret law, for example, doesn't have a 'good faith' exception. Disclosing a trade secret, even in good faith, can still destroy its protected status.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Uniform Trade Secrets Act (Cal. Civ. Code § 3426)
perturbation.law_explanation: The California Uniform Trade Secrets Act defines trade secrets and provides remedies for misappropriation. There's no 'good faith' exception; the act focuses on whether reasonable efforts were made to maintain secrecy and whether the information was improperly acquired. The added clause could create ambiguity and weaken trade secret protection.

GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426. This title may be cited as the Uniform Trade Secrets Act. (Added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1.)
perturbation.justification: The addition of "Confidential Information does not include information shared in good faith" creates a legal contradiction regarding trade secret protection. The Law Explanation and Perturbation Explanation both state that trade secret law does not have a 'good faith' exception, and focusing on maintaining secrecy. Even if disclosed 'in good faith,' the protected status of the trade secret is destroyed. Scraped Law Snippet 1 states that Title 5 of the Civil Code is the Uniform Trade Secrets Act, implying that misappropriation of trade secrets is actionable regardless of intent. Thus, the modified contract introduces an ambiguity that weakens the protection afforded to trade secrets, creating a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 5. Upon termination or expiration of the Agreement, or upon written request of the other party, each party shall promptly destroy or return to the other all documents and other tangible materials representing the other's Confidential Information and all copies thereof. The Recipient agrees to destroy all documents, memoranda, notes and other writings whatsoever prepared by the Recipient or its employees or representatives based on the information contained in the Confidential Information (except for references or summaries appearing in minutes or corporate records).
perturbation.changed_text: 5. Upon termination or expiration of the Agreement, or upon written request of the other party, each party shall promptly destroy or return to the other all documents and other tangible materials representing the other's Confidential Information and all copies thereof. The Recipient agrees to destroy all documents, memoranda, notes and other writings whatsoever prepared by the Recipient or its employees or representatives based on the information contained in the Confidential Information (except for references or summaries appearing in minutes or corporate records), unless such records are required for compliance with Sarbanes-Oxley.
perturbation.explanation: This adds an exception for Sarbanes-Oxley compliance to the destruction of confidential information. While it seems reasonable, it creates a potential conflict. SOX requires maintaining certain financial records, but doesn't automatically override confidentiality agreements about other types of information commingled in those records. It might necessitate keeping records that should otherwise be destroyed under the NDA, potentially violating the NDA.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Sarbanes-Oxley Act of 2002 (Specifically, document retention requirements)
perturbation.law_explanation: While SOX mandates the retention of certain financial records, it doesn't explicitly address how those requirements interact with pre-existing confidentiality agreements. Adding a blanket exception for 'Sarbanes-Oxley compliance' creates a loophole, potentially forcing a party to violate the NDA to comply with document retention under SOX, when SOX does not require maintaining the specific confidential info.

Definitions Except as otherwise specifically provided in this Act, in this Act, the following definitions shall apply: (1) Appropriate State regulatory authority The term "appropriate State regulatory authority" means the State agency or other authority responsible for the licensure or other regulation of the practice of accounting in the State or States having jurisdiction over a registered public accounting firm or associated

perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces an exception to the destruction of confidential information for 'Sarbanes-Oxley compliance.' The law explanation states that SOX does not explicitly address how its retention requirements interact with pre-existing confidentiality agreements. The added exception creates a potential loophole where a party might be forced to violate the NDA to comply with document retention under SOX, even when SOX doesn't mandate retaining the specific confidential information covered by the NDA. This misalignment in terminology creates a legal contradiction as the contract's language deviates from legally recognized definitions, potentially making the contract unenforceable.

perturbation.original_text: 17. Neither party shall export, directly or indirectly, any technical data acquired from the other pursuant to this Agreement or any product utilizing any 3 <PAGE> 4 such data to any country for which the U.S. Government or any agency thereof at the time of export requires an export license or other governmental approval without first obtaining such license or approval.
perturbation.changed_text: 17. Neither party shall export, directly or indirectly, any technical data acquired from the other pursuant to this Agreement or any product utilizing any 3 <PAGE> 4 such data to any country unless that country is a member of the World Trade Organization.
perturbation.explanation: This change replaces the requirement for obtaining necessary US government export licenses with a blanket allowance for exports to WTO member countries. This is a problem because export controls are country-specific and cover more than just WTO members. Some WTO members may still be subject to export restrictions for national security reasons or other policy considerations.
perturbation.law_explanation: The EAR controls the export and reexport of most commercial items. Export licenses may be required based on the item, the destination, the end-user, and the end-use. The modification contradicts the EAR by allowing exports to all WTO members without regard to these specific licensing requirements. Many items may still need a license even if the destination is a WTO member.

The Electronic Code of Federal Regulations (eCFR) is a continuously updated online version of the CFR. It is not an official legal edition of the CFR. Learn more about the eCFR, its status, and the editorial process. Commerce Control List (CCL) The Commerce Control List (CCL) (Supplement No. 1 to Part 774 of the EAR) is divided into ten broad categories, and each category is further subdivided into five product groups. Additionally, you may explore our interactive tool . Category 0 - Nuclear Materials Facilities & Equipment [and Miscellaneous Items] Category 1 - Special Materials and Related Equipment, Chemicals, “Microorganisms,” and “Toxins” Category 2 - Materials Processing Category 3 - Electronics Category 4 - Computers Category 5 Part 1 - Telecommunications Category 5 Part 2 - Information Security Category 6 - Sensors and Lasers Category 7 - Navigation and Avionics Category 8 - Marine Category 9 - Aerospace and Propulsion Legal limitations of the tools on the BIS website The tools on the BIS website are not intended to replace the official rules found in the Code of Federal Regulations , nor are they a primer on how to get started. For that introductory guidance, please visit our Learn and Support page. The tools are an editorial compilation of material and not an official legal edition of the Code of Federal Regulations or the Federal Register . We have made every effort to ensure the material presented in these tools are accurate, but if you are relying on them for legal research you should consult the official editions of those sources to confirm your findings. Nothing in these tools binds BIS or creates any rights, benefits, or defenses, substantive or procedural, that are enforceable by any party in any manner. If you find any errors, please email us.
perturbation.justification: The modified text creates a legal contradiction by replacing the requirement for obtaining U.S. government export licenses with a blanket allowance for exports to WTO member countries. The original text adheres to U.S. export control regulations, while the changed text undermines them by suggesting that WTO membership is sufficient for export compliance. The Law Explanation states that export licenses may be required based on the item, destination, end-user, and end-use. Scraped Law Snippet 1 refers to the Export Administration Regulations (EAR) under 15 CFR 730-774, which controls the export and reexport of most commercial items, indicating that specific licensing requirements exist beyond WTO membership. The modified text disregards these specific requirements, leading to a clear non-compliance with export regulations. Scraped Law Snippet 2 mentions the Commerce Control List (CCL), further emphasizing that export controls involve item-specific considerations, irrespective of WTO status. The change removes the core legal obligation to comply with U.S. export licensing requirements, making the contractual terms unenforceable where they conflict with export regulations.
